Description: Token-based authorization system for gating AI agent access to host applications.
Create, store, and manage the lifecycle of access and refresh tokens.
Issue a new access token and refresh token with specified scopes and access tiers.
Exchange a valid refresh token for a new access/refresh token pair, revoking the old refresh token.
Revoke all access and refresh tokens belonging to a token family.
Add, remove, or change the access tier of scopes on an existing token family.
Revoke an existing token family and issue a new one with the same scopes.
Return metadata about a token including scopes, expiry, and family membership.
Detect when a consumed refresh token is reused and revoke the entire token family.
Verify token authenticity and check scope authorization.
Validate the HMAC signature of a token against the signing key.
Verify that a token has not expired.
Verify that a token carries the required scope for an operation.
Determine whether a scope is allow, prompt, or deny for a given token.
Request and manage human approval for prompt-tier operations via a pluggable notification system.
Hold a request pending and invoke the configured notification plugin to request user approval. The plugin is configured in agent-auth's configuration file (desktop notification by default).
Load and initialize the configured notification plugin from agent-auth's configuration file.
Record an approval decision with its grant type (once or timed).
Check whether a prior approval grant covers the current request.
Remove approval grants whose time-boxed duration has elapsed.
Manage cryptographic keys stored in the system keyring.
Generate a new HMAC signing key in the system keyring on first use.
Load the HMAC signing key from the system keyring for token signing and verification.
Generate a new AES-256-GCM encryption key in the system keyring on first use.
Load the encryption key from the system keyring for field-level encryption of sensitive database columns.
Persist token families and tokens in SQLite with field-level encryption for sensitive columns.
Write a new token family record with its scopes (encrypted) to the database.
Write a new access or refresh token record to the database, encrypting the HMAC signature.
Mark a refresh token as consumed after use.
Mark a token family as revoked, invalidating all its tokens.
Read token and family records for validation, introspection, and listing, decrypting sensitive fields.
Encrypt a column value using AES-256-GCM with the database encryption key.
Decrypt a column value using AES-256-GCM with the database encryption key.
Expose agent-auth functionality over HTTP for use by app bridges and CLIs. All endpoints are prefixed with /agent-auth/.
Handle POST /agent-auth/validate requests from app bridges, including blocking for JIT approval on prompt-tier scopes.
Handle POST /agent-auth/token/refresh requests from CLIs.
Handle POST /agent-auth/token/reissue requests from CLIs when the refresh token has expired. Triggers JIT approval via the configured notification plugin.
Handle GET /agent-auth/token/status requests for token introspection.
Record all token operations and authorization decisions for security review.
Record token creation, refresh, reissue, revocation, rotation, and scope modification events.
Record validation results including scope checks, tier resolutions, and JIT approval outcomes.
Provide command-line token management for the host user.
Parse arguments and invoke token pair creation.
Display all active token families and their scopes.
Parse arguments and invoke scope modification on an existing token family.
Parse arguments and invoke token family revocation.
Parse arguments and invoke token family rotation.
Start the agent-auth HTTP server.
Expose authenticated Things 3 read operations over HTTP to the CLI client, delegating authorization to agent-auth.
Forward the bearer token to agent-auth for validation, scope checking, and any JIT approval before executing an operation.
Retrieve the todos, projects, or areas requested by the client, translating typed errors into the ThingsError hierarchy.
Expose Things 3 read operations as HTTP endpoints for the CLI client under the /things-bridge/ prefix.
Read-only Things 3 client CLI that shells to osascript. Ships with the distribution and is the bridge's default things_client_command.
Interact with the Things 3 application via AppleScript / osascript and return structured results. Owns AppleScript generation, TSV parsing, and the 30s per-call osascript timeout.
Expose the shared read surface (todos / projects / areas sub-commands) and emit the JSON-on-stdout + exit-code envelope that the things-bridge subprocess contract requires.
Test-only in-memory Things client CLI that reads a YAML fixture. Never shipped; invoked by the bridge during integration/e2e tests.

Answer the shared Things-client CLI surface from an in-memory store seeded from a YAML fixture. Enables end-to-end exercise of the agent-auth + things-bridge + things-cli stack on Linux without osascript or Things 3.
Thin CLI client for interacting with Things 3 via the things-bridge, runnable from host or devcontainer.
Send an authenticated HTTP request to the app bridge with the bearer token.
Detect 401 responses, refresh the token pair via agent-auth, and retry the request. If the refresh token has expired, attempt re-issuance via JIT approval.
Persist and load credentials using the system keyring (macOS Keychain or libsecret/gnome-keyring), with plaintext file fallback via --credential-store=file.
Parse CLI arguments and map subcommands to bridge HTTP requests.
Format and print structured results from the bridge to the terminal.
